coredata.dc:title: Boswellia ovalifoliolata bark extract derived carbon dots for selective fluorescent sensing of Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Venkatesan, Geetha
Rajagopalan, Vijayaraghavan
Chakravarthula, Sarada Nallani
coredata.dc:description: A facile, efficient, green synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) using Boswellia ovalifoliolata bark extract by hydrothermal-carbonization method. Synthesized CDs were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, Scanning electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, Raman spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, Spectrofluorophotometry, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. The synthesized CDs exhibit excitation dependent emission, also excellent water solubility due to the presence of hydroxyl functional groups on the surface. The maximum fluorescence emission peak observed at 460 nm for the excitation at 360 nm. The obtained CDs possess high stability in aqueous solution and exhibit strong green emission with 10.2% quantum yield. The average size of the CDs was approximately 4.16 ± 0.22 nm. The synthesized CDs shows high sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+ with a linear relationship up to the concentration of 500 μM with a detection limit of 0.41 μM (S/N = 3). The CDs also possess excellent free radical scavenging activity with IC50 value of 65 μg/mL. The overall results suggest that synthesized CDs from natural carbon source could be utilized for Fe3+ sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fe3+ detection
Fluorescence
Boswellia ovalifoliolata
Sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of B. ovalifoliolata bark extract and the synthesized CDs.
(a) HRTEM image with 10 nm magnification, (b) SEM image, (c) XRD pattern and (d) Raman spectrum of synthesized CDs.
(a) XPS spectra of the B. ovalifoliolata CDs (b) C1s (c) O1s.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in different concentration of NaCl and (b) fluorescence dependence of CDs on the variation of ionic strength (c) excitation dependent fluorescence emission spectra and (d) excitation dependent maximum emission wavelength, (e) pH dependent fluorescence emission spectra and (f) corresponding graph of CDs.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CD in the presence of various anions at a concentration level of 10 mmol L−1 and bar diagram for the variation of fluorescence intensities (λex/λem = 360 nm/460 nm) of CDs in presence of various anions.

(a) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs in the presence of different metal ions and (b) the corresponding bar diagram, (c) different concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 500 μM and (d) selectivity investigation (F0/F)/F0 of CDs, (e) concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 140 μM and (f) selectivity investigation (F0/F)/F0 of CDs.
Fluorescence decay curve of synthesized CDs and CDs + Fe3+.
List of fluorescent carbon dots from different carbon source for Fe3+ detection.

head.ce:title: Boswellia ovalifoliolata bark extract derived carbon dots for selective fluorescent sensing of Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, VIT University, Vellore, 632014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile, efficient, green synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) using Boswellia ovalifoliolata bark extract by hydrothermal-carbonization method. Synthesized CDs were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, Scanning electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, Raman spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, Spectrofluorophotometry, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. The synthesized CDs exhibit excitation dependent emission, also excellent water solubility due to the presence of hydroxyl functional groups on the surface. The maximum fluorescence emission peak observed at 460 nm for the excitation at 360 nm. The obtained CDs possess high stability in aqueous solution and exhibit strong green emission with 10.2% quantum yield. The average size of the CDs was approximately 4.16 ± 0.22 nm. The synthesized CDs shows high sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+ with a linear relationship up to the concentration of 500 μM with a detection limit of 0.41 μM (S/N = 3). The CDs also possess excellent free radical scavenging activity with IC50 value of 65 μg/mL. The overall results suggest that synthesized CDs from natural carbon source could be utilized for Fe3+ sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrial evolution with increasing human population leads to release of large amount of heavy metals and transition metal ions. It is widely affect the environmental safety and human health. For this reason, researchers are keen to develop a fluorescent sensor with highly selective and sensitive towards the detection of heavy metal ions. Ferric ion (Fe3+) is one of the most important transition metal in living organism, biological systems and most abundant metal cation found in natural environment. The Fe3+ involve in numerous biological processes like catalytic process, electron transfer, exchange and transport of oxygen, and enzymatic reaction at cellular level. Additionally, the accumulation of excess iron in human body via food chains causes several diseases such as Alzheimer’s disease, cancer and Parkinson’s syndrome. The detection of Fe3+in presence of other ions such as K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Cr3+ and Co2+ is very important because it play a major role in industrial pollution and also in health and disease. Thus, simple cost effective and green CDs have been developed for the sensing of metal ions especially Fe3+ sensor as the variation may cause diseases such as anemia, heart failure, diabetes, intelligence decline and many more [1–10]. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency has fixed maximum limits of Fe3+ metal ion in drinking water and food to 1.3 ppm (∼20 μM) [11]. The traditional analytical techniques for detecting Fe3+ are optical sensor, mass spectrometry, dye based sensor, inductive coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS), metal organic frameworks, inductive coupled mass atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES), noble metal quantum clusters, ion chromatography and plasmon resonance Raleigh scattering (PRRS) spectroscopy but these probes are complex and time consuming synthetic routes and contain toxic or expensive reagents. Thus, it is necessary to explore the CDs which has high selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ sensing.

Various fluorescent material are used for detecting metal ions such as carbon dots, organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots, polymers, nanoparticles and small molecules. Among these carbon quantum dots or carbon fluorescent dots having attractive features such as strong photoluminescence (PL), optical properties, multicolored fluorescence, environmental friendliness, high chemical stability, good conductivity, low toxicity, broad band optical absorption, surface tunable functionalities, and easy to synthesis at large scale with low cost [1,2,12–18].

Carbon dots (CDs) are generally defined as carbon nanomaterials (sizes < 10 nm) is a new member of carbonaceous material family and they are accidently discovered during the separation and purification of single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) in 2004 by Xu et al. [19–22]. Carbon quantum dots or fluorescent carbon nanoparticles received their name by Sun et al. in 2006 and they followed synthetic route with much enhanced fluorescence emission through surface passivation. A variety of methods have been developed to synthesis CDs like arc discharge, electrochemical oxidation, laser ablation, solvothermal, electrochemical oxidation, oxygen plasma treatment and microwave irradiation [23–28]. Among all these reported methods, hydrothermal carbonization has more advantage over existing physical methods, which may be due to the simplicity and production of CDs with good quantum yield. There are several materials reported in the literatures for the synthesis of CDs by hydrothermal carbonization from different natural sources like potato, honey, milk, bagasse, bovine serum albumin, gelatin, pomelo peels, willow bark, pipe tobacco, Bombyx mori silk, chitosan, grape juice, papaya powder, soy milk and bamboo leaves which could be a probes for sensing several chemical species and cells in vitro and in vivo [13,14,25,29]. Thus, hydrothermal carbonization is an eco-friendly, classical and facile route for the synthesis of CDs in aqueous media. Carbon dots has various applications in chemical sensing, biosensing, bioimaging, nanomedicine, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis. Among these application the chemical sensing of CDs for the detection of metal ions have attracted due to their high quantum yield, low cost, low toxicity, easy fabrication, tunable emission, good photostability and environmental friendliness. But in some of the green preparation of CDs face serious problem such as low sensitivity, poor selectivity, low quantum yield, use of organic solvents and basic environment [3,4,13,25,30]. It is essential to prepare CDs with easily available carbon source, environmental friendly and has high quantum yield.

In recent times, green chemistry has more attention because of its clean, economical and sustain features. So, there is an urgent need to prepare carbon source from economical, facile and simple green method for the synthesis of CDs. Hence, Boswellia ovalifoliolata bark have been used to synthesis fluorescent carbon dots. Boswellia ovalifoliolata (N.P.Balakr. & A.N.Henry) is a medicinal tree species belongs to the family burseraceae and a habitat of seshachalam hill range of eastern ghats of India. It is widely used by the tribal people like nakkala, sugali and chenchu to treat various medical ailments [31,32]. In this article, a facile and green method for the preparation of carbon dots from Boswellia ovalifoliolata bark have been used for the first time as a carbon source for synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots. Furthermore, the prepared carbon dots revealed excellent radical scavenging activity and further it can be used in biological applications. In addition, the CDs showed highly sensitive and selective fluorescence quenching effect upon addition of Fe3+ ions with limit of detection of 0.41 μM (S/N = 3) and the fluorescence emission of carbon dots quenched 99% efficiently after the addition of Fe3+ at 460 nm and thus it can serve as an effective fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ ions. Hence the synthesized carbon dots is highly selective, sensitive, low cost and efficient in sensing Fe3+ ions.

B. Ovalifoliolata bark were collected from Seshachalam hills in Andhra Pradesh, India. The chemical like boric acid are obtained from Thermo Fischer Scientific India Pvt. Ltd. Mumbai, India. The stock solution of different metal cations, metal anions were prepared by metal chlorides or nitrates, e.g., NaHSO3, NaHSO4, KIO3, sodium acetate, sodium citrate, acetic acid, phosphoric acid, MgCl2, MnCl2, ZnCl2, CdCl2, FeCl2, Pb(NO3)2, NaCl, KCl, CO(NO3)2, CrCl3, AlCl3, FeCl3, NiCl2, EDTA, NaHCO3 and NaNO2 were purchased from SD-fine chem, Mumbai, India. All chemicals used were of analytical grade and de-ionized water was used throughout the experiments.

The collected B. ovalifoliolata bark was washed thoroughly with running tap water and was made into fine powder using mixer grinder. The fluorescent CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using B. ovalifoliolata as a carbon source. About 5 g of B. ovalifoliolata bark was mixed with distilled water and filtered through Whatman filter paper. The bark extract (50 mL) was transferred to a 100 mL poly (tetrafluoro ethylene) Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave for hydrothermal treatment and heated at 180 °C for 5 h in an oven. The resultant dark brown solution in the autoclave was cooled down naturally to room temperature. The obtained dark brown solution was subjected to centrifugation (13,000 rpm, 20 min) and filtered through a Whatman filter paper. The filtered solution was subjected to membrane filter paper (0.22 μm) to remove larger particles. The resultant solution was lyophilized and 0.62 g of dry powder was obtained, in 12.4% yield [33]. The carbon dots were dispersed in water at a concentration of 1 mg/mL for further characterization and use [33].

High-Resolution Transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) were recorded by using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), (Model JSM 6390 L V, JOEL, USA). A drop of CDs solution was coated over the surface of the copper grid and the grid was dried. The surface morphology and particle size of the synthesized nanoparticles were examined using SEM using Carl Zeiss EVO 18, Germany. For SEM analysis, the samples were coated in coverslips. The UV–vis absorption spectra were determined using a JASCO (Great Dunmow, UK) V-670 spectrophotometer in the absorption wavelength of 200–800 nm at room temperature. The Infrared (IR) spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu (Japan) Infrared spectrophotometer (400–4000 cm−1) with resolution IV. Raman spectroscopy analysis was carried out using AZILTRON, PRO 532 (USA) with 532 nm laser excitation source. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies were recorded using a XPS microprobe (PHI 5000 versa probe-ULVAC-PHI Inc.). X-ray diffraction (XRD) method using Bruker D8 advanced through Ni-filtered Cu Kα (λ = 0.15406 nm) with the range of 2θ = 10–90°. The fluorescent behaviour of the CDs was measured by using a Hitachi (Tokyo, Japan) F-7000 FL fluorescence spectrophotometer with 150 W Xe lamp as the excitation source and was recorded in different excitation wavelength (280, 320, 340, 360, 380, 400, 420 and 440 nm).

The Quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized CDs were determined by using 0.1 mol L−1 quinine sulphate solution in H2SO4 as the reference. The fluorescence quantum yield (Փ fl) was calculated by following Eq. (1).
(1)
Փ fl = Փ s × (F/F s ) × (A s /A) × (n/n s )2
where Φs is the reported quantum yield of the reference, F and Fs are the integrated area of sample and reference solutions, A and As are the absorbance of sample and reference, and n is the refractive index with 1.33 as the default for both quinine sulphate and CDs. [34]

The fluorescence behaviour and stability of the obtained CDs were investigated in different buffer solutions (0.04 mol L−1 of boric acid, acetic acid, phosphoric acid, and sodium hydroxide). The influence of ionic strength in the presence of various concentrations of NaCl (1.0 mol L−1) was evaluated. The influence of different cationic and anionic species on fluorescence behaviour of CDs was investigated. The fluorescence stability of different metal salts at a concentration level of 10 mmol L−1 was examined. The carbon dots are excited at 360 nm and fluorescence emission at 460 nm with the slit of 5 nm was recorded.

The fluorescence emission of the synthesized CDs was measured at excitation wavelength of 360 nm and strong emission wavelength at 460 nm. The strong fluorescence emission of the carbon dots decreased upon addition of different metal ions. There is no change in the fluorescence emission of CDs before addition of metal ions. The detection of Fe3+ ions was performed using 1 mg mL−1 of CDs and the addition of standard Fe3+ ions in different concentrations. The sensitivity and selectivity of CDs in sensing different metal ions (Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Cr3+ and Co2+) was measured by mixing the aqueous metal ions stock solution with the concentration of 100 μM into the CDs solution. The fluorescence intensity of CDs without the addition of any metal ions was measured and set as F0 (blank/control). The synthesized CDs showed high sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+ ions than other metal ions by quenching the fluorescence emission of the carbon dots. The synthesized CDs highly sensed Fe3+ metal ions and further the detection was carried out at lower concentration to determine the limit of detection.

The DPPH radical scavenging activity of CDs was evaluated by the reduction of DPPH induced by the addition of CDs in methanol solution. About 10 μL of CDs was added to 2 mL of DPPH (100 μM) solution. The decrease in absorption at 517 nm was measured. The percentage inhibition was calculated by using the following Eq. (2).
(2)
Inhibition
%
=
A c
-A
A c
100 %
Where, Ac and As refers to the absorbance of the DPPH free radicals at 517 nm in the absence and presence of CDs respectively.

Fluorescence microscopy was used to evaluate the bioimaging application of the B. ovalifoliolata carbon dots and to assess the labelling capability of the nanoparticle. The nanoparticle was prepared and suspended in PBS pH 7.4 (0.1 M) and the nanoparticle fluorescence images were recorded using Fluorescence microscope (FM 3000, Wesmox, India).

The CDs were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method using B. ovalifoliolata bark extract as a carbon source, since the bark extract contains large number of terpenoids, polyphenols, tannins, anthocyanin and steroids with polar functional groups such as -OH, -COOH and -NH2, providing the carbon source for the formation of strong green fluorescent emission under UV light at 360 nm with high quantum yield. The organic molecules present in the bark extract converted into CDs by hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 5 h. The excellent polar solubility infers that CDs surface consists of polar functional group wrapped by multiple polar surface functional groups. The synthesized CDs were utilized for detection of Fe3+ ions. The synthesized CDs were characterized by FT-IR, XRD, TEM, SEM, Raman spectrum, PL and UV–vis spectrophotometer analysis.

FT-IR analysis was used to investigate the functional groups present in the CDs surface. FT-IR spectra of B. ovalifoliolata bark powder, water soluble carbon dots indicated that CDs possess surface carboxyl groups, methyl group and phenolic group that can be used for sensor applications. The broad absorption band at 3314 cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibration of OH; the weak absorption band at 2940 cm-1 associated with CH stretching vibration. CO and CH bending vibration are found at 1107, 1443 and 1032 cm−1. The peak at 1693 cm-1 ascribed to NH bending vibration as shown in Fig. 1 [10]. The Horiba nanoparticle analyser was used to determine the charge and stability of CDs dispersions. Zeta potential was determined for prepared CDs and the value was found to be −29.0 mV (Fig. S1). The synthesized CDs showed negative zeta potential value, which may be due to the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs [35,36].

The TEM image of CDs is shown in Fig. 2 a. The HR-TEM image indicates that the CDs are spherical in shape and well dispersed without any aggregation. The average particle size of the synthesized particle was found to be 4.16 ± 0.22 nm and the lattice spacing is 0.25 nm. The selected area electron diffraction pattern of the CDs revealed that the CDs were amorphous in nature and it is in agreement with the results [13–15]. SEM analysis was carried out to determine the actual shape of the synthesized carbon dots. The SEM image clearly shows that the CDs are spherical in nature as shown in Fig. 2b [37]. A typical X-ray diffraction pattern Fig. 2c of synthesized CDs shows one broad peak centred at 2θ = 40.4 which is due to the presence of graphitic carbon and shoulder peak at 20.7 corresponds to the (311) plane of carbon which is consistent with the previous report for CDs. The weak peak at 2θ = 41.6 corresponds to the plane of (200) [12,15]. The Raman spectrum of the carbon dots as shown in Fig. 2d indicates the graphite nature of the carbon present in the synthesized CDs. The Raman spectrum shows two peaks at 1321 and 1518 cm−1 corresponding to the D (disordered sp3-hybridized carbon) and G band (ordered sp2-hybridized carbon) respectively. The sharp intense peak observed at G-band 1518 cm−1 and the weak peak observed at d-band 1321 cm−1 reveals the presence of carbon nanomaterials in CDs. The relative intensity ratio ID/IG values were calculated to be 0.87 (sp3/sp2) indicates the purity of the CDs. The Raman results suggest purity of the prepared CDs and are amorphous in nature [12,19,23].

The synthesized CDs were characterized to investigate chemical composition and the surface state of the CDs. The synthesized CDs shows XPS scan with three elements such as carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen (Fig. 3 a). The XPS spectrum shows three typical peaks of C1s (283.9 eV), N1s (398.5 eV) and O1s (531.6 eV) and the content was 69.1, 0.4 and 30.5%. High resolution of C1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) revealed the presence of three peaks at 284.4 eV, 285.4 eV and 286.3 eV corresponding to CC/CC, CN, CO and CO bonds. The binding energy peak at 283.9 eV indicated that the synthesized CDs possessed sp2 carbons. XPS analysis of O1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) revealed the presence of two peaks at 531.9 eV and 532.9 eV, which was attributed to COH/COC and CO, respectively. The N1s spectrum did not show a prominent peak in the region, as the elemental composition is very less [1,5,24].

The optical properties of the synthesized CDs confirming the UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra in aqueous solution (water). The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs showed a sharp absorption peak at 280 nm corresponding to the n-π* transition for aromatic sp2 hybridization of CO bond. The emission spectra showed a strong green emission with maximum emission wavelength at 460 nm could be observed under excitation at 360 nm. The diluted CDs in aqueous solution is brownish yellow in color under ambient light but exhibits an intense green color when irradiated with a UV-light (λ = 360 nm) indicating the green fluorescent property of the CDs. The absorption peak at 280 nm is from the trapping of excited state energy of the surface states, which can lead to strong fluorescence. The fluorescence emission of CDs may be due to their surface energy trap, electron-hole recombination, quantum size effect, aromatic structures and edge effects.

The synthesized CDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission character. The fluorescence emission peaks red shifted from 400 nm to 535 nm by varying the excitation wavelength from 275 nm to 460 nm. The strong fluorescence emission spectrum at 460 nm was obtained at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the increase in the excitation wavelength from 275 nm to 360 nm as shown in Fig. 4 a and b, and then gradually decreased with increased excitation wavelength from 380 nm to 440 nm. The excitation dependent maximum emission wavelength is given in Fig. 4b. The excitation-wavelength dependent exhibited difference in the fluorescence intensity which may be due to the different particle sizes and diverse surface energy trap sites [1].

The stability of the synthesized CDs was studied by adding different concentrations of NaCl (0–1.0 mol L−1) to the CDs solution. The fluorescence emission intensity of CDs as shown in Fig. 4c and d, did not change much upon addition of different concentration of NaCl which was beneficial for the CDs to be used in practical applications in presence of various salt concentrations. The effect of pH as shown in Fig. 4e and f, was also studied for the CDs to check the fluorescence behaviour of the CDs in different pH value. The fluorescence emission intensity was enhanced with the increase of pH values from 8.0 to 12.0. The fluorescence intensity was decreased with the decrease of pH values. The pH-dependent fluorescence behaviour is due to the electron donors and electron acceptors character of synthesized CDs. However, the presence of H+ or OH- may interrupt or inhibit the electron transfer process [26]. The effect of various anionic species at a certain concentration (10 mmol L−1) was also studied to check the stability of the CDs. There is no much change in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs on the addition of various anionic species (CH3COO-, HSO3-, HSO4-, HCO3-, BO4-, IO3-, NO3-) as shown in Fig. 5 . The results are favourable for the treatment and sensing of CDs in biological systems 6 [5,6].

The strong fluorescence emission of the synthesized CDs could be explored as a chemosensor for sensing metal ions. The ability of synthesized fluorescent CDs to sense different metal ions by fluorescent quenching method was evaluated and found that Fe3+ can be able to quench the fluorescence of CDs completely. To evaluate the selectivity of CDs towards Fe3+, the fluorescence quenching was investigated (Fig. 6 a). It is from the figure that carbon dots emit strong fluorescence at 460 nm. The figure also depicts the quenching of the fluorescence by different metal ions. The fluorescence emission of CDs quenched 99% after the addition of Fe3+. The result suggested that the CD had exceptional selectivity towards Fe3+ and the other metal don’t show significant quenching. The bar diagram (Fig. 6b) clearly indicates that the quenching by ions with three to four times higher than that of the other ions. The fluorescence titrations were performed for the sensitivity of the synthesized CDs towards the sensing of Fe3+. The fluorescence titration (Fig. 6c) of the CDs with different Fe3+ concentration confirmed the linear correlation (R2 = 0.997). A linear calibration graph as shown in Fig. 6d was obtained by plotting Fe3+ concentration against versus difference of fluorescence, i.e., (F0 – F)/F0 , where F and F0 represents the fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+ as shown in Fig. 6e and f. The limit of detection of Fe3+ was calculated based on Eq. (3) and was found to be 0.41 μM. these results suggest that the synthesized CDs can detect Fe3+ in very low concentrations (0.41 μM) in water by fluorescence quenching method and showed highest quenching value of 0.99 (F0 – F)/ F0 for Fe3+ compare to other metal ion [4–10,38].
(3)
LOD =
3σ Slope
Where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signals (n = 3)

The surface of the CDs contains oxygen containing functional groups such as –OH and –COOH, which facilitates the formation of complex with Fe3+. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CDs by the addition of Fe3+ may be due to the non-radiative electron transfer between Fe3+ and phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CDs. Due to this interaction, the electron in the excited state of CDs may transfer to the unfilled orbit of Fe3+ and lead to non-radiative electron transfer which results in the fluorescence quenching of CDs. The fluorescence emission intensity of CDs decreases gradually on increasing the Fe3+ concentration. Thus, the non-radiative electron transfer causing fluorescence quenching of CDs as follows in Eq. (4) and the synthesized CDs reveals that sensing system is selective and sensitive to Fe3+ concentration [1,2,38].
(4) 6CD – OH + Fe3+→ [Fe(OCD)6]3+ + 6H+.

To know the mechanism of quenching, fluorescence lifetime of CDs was carried out in the presence and absence of Fe3+ as shown in Fig. 7 . The fluorescent lifetime study used to differentiate whether static or dynamic quenching process. In static quenching there is no changes in the lifetime due to complexation between non-fluorescent fluorophore and quenching agent in the ground state. In the case of dynamic quenching the fluorescent lifetime will be affected in excited state due to electron transfer from excited fluorophore to the quenching agent. The pristine CDs exhibited lifetime of 4.2 ns and it was slightly decreased after addition of Fe3+ (3.8 ns). This results confirms that fluorescent quenching of CDs by Fe3+ is due to dynamic quenching process by non-radiative electron transfer [39,40].

Table 1 shows the list of fluorescent carbon dots synthesized from green and chemical method for Fe3+ detection reported in the literature with the detection limit and their synthesis process. It can be seen that the CDs synthesized from Boswellia ovalifoliolata bark extracts endowed with low detection limit and can be applicable for Fe3+ detection.

The antioxidant property of the CDs was determined by DPPH radical scavenging assay method. Ascorbic was used as a standard. The solution colour was changed from deep purple to light yellow colour. The percentage inhibition of synthesized CDs was measured at 517 nm using UV–vis spectrophotometer against control and the percentage inhibition was calculated. The addition of 20 μg/mL of CDs to DPPH methanol solution, decreases the absorbance at 517 nm in UV–vis spectrophotometer as shown in Fig. S2a. The absorbance of DPPH was gradually decreased on increasing the concentration of CDs and saturated at 200 μg/mL. Further, the addition of CDs could not cause much change in the absorbance spectra. The minimum inhibitory concentration IC50 value of CDs were found the 65 μg/mL as shown in Fig. S2b and for the standard ascorbic acid were about 2 μg/mL as shown in Fig. S2c. the CDs can be able to scavenge the free radicals by DPPH assay method and can act as an excellent antioxidant reagent [20].

The fluorescence nature of the carbon dots can be utilized for the bio imaging of cell line application. The carbon dots were treated with PBS [42] to evaluate the fluorescence property of the particle and the sample was placed on a microscope slide to acquire the fluorescence image. The carbon dots exhibited and green fluorescence Fig. S3. The photoluminescence measurements were conducted using long pass filters [43]. The obtained results indicate that carbon dots can also act as bioimaging probes [44–47].

In summary, we are successfully synthesized CDs from Boswellia ovalifoliolata bark extract by simple hydrothermal method. The average size was found to be 4.16 ± 0.22 nm, which exhibit good water solubility, excitation dependent fluorescence emission wavelength, stable and strong fluorescence without any modification in the surface under different pH and certain species of anions. The synthesized CDs reveals good quantum yield of 10.2% and it can be used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution with high sensitivity and selectivity with detection limit of 0.41 μM. The prepared CDs also reveal favourable free radical scavenging activity with IC50 value of 65 μg/mL. To our knowledge, this is the first reported method to synthesize the fluorescent CDs from Boswellia ovalifoliolata bark extract and further they can be applicable for bioimaging and metal ion sensing. Thus, the synthesized carbon dots is highly selective, sensitive, efficient, and cost effective and it can be effectively used for sensing metal ions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank VIT University, Vellore for providing facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jece.2019.103013.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots using Chionanthus retusus fruit extract and investigation of their suitability for metal ion sensing and biological applications
Facile synthesis of fluorescence carbon dots from sweet potato for Fe3+ sensing and cell imaging
Rapid solid-phase microwave synthesis of highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for Fe3+detection and cellular bioimaging
Synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen and phosphorus doped carbon dots for the detection of Fe3+ ions in cancer cells
Green preparation of carbon dots with papaya as carbon source for effective fluorescent sensing of Iron (III) and Escherichia coli
Fluorescence sensor array based on amino acid derived carbon dots for pattern-based detection of toxic metal ions
Turn-off fluorescence sensor for the detection of ferric ion in water using green synthesized N-doped carbon dots and its bio-imaging
Comparative study for N and S doped carbon dots: synthesis, characterization and applications for Fe3+ probe and cellular imaging
Dual functional N- and S-co-doped carbon dots as the sensor for temperature and Fe3+ ions
An ionic liquid promoted microwave-hydrothermal route towards highly photoluminescent carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of iron(iii)
Green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from sugarcane bagasse pulp
Green approach to photoluminescent carbon dots for imaging of gram-negative bacteria Escherichia coli
A simplistic approach to green future with eco-friendly luminescent carbon dots and their application to fluorescent nano-sensor ‘turn-off’ probe for selective sensing of copper ions
Green synthesis of carbon dots from prawn shells for highly selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Strongly blue-luminescent N-doped carbogenic dots as a tracer metal sensing probe in aqueous medium and its potential activity towards in situ Ag-nanoparticle synthesis
Comparative solvent extraction study of silver(I) by MEHPA and Cyanex 302 as acidic extractants in a new industrial diluent (MIPS)
Waste candle soot derived nitrogen doped carbon dots based fluorescent sensor probe: an efficient and inexpensive route to determine Hg(II) and Fe(III) from water
outright green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from eutrophic algal blooms for in vitro imaging
Green synthesis of bifunctional fluorescent carbon dots from garlic for cellular imaging and free radical scavenging
Carbon dots: large-scale synthesis, sensing and bioimaging
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Green synthesis of carbon dots from rose-heart radish and application for Fe3+ detection and cell imaging
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots for selective detection of tartrazine in food samples
Nitrogen-doping enhanced fluorescent carbon dots: green synthesis and their applications for bioimaging and label-free detection of Au3+ ions
Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from lotus root for Hg(II) ions detection and cell imaging
Fluorescent carbon dots and their sensing applications
Economical and green synthesis of bagasse-derived fluorescent carbon dots for biomedical applications
Ethanolic extract of Boswellia ovalifoliolata bark and leaf attenuates doxorubicin-induced cardiotoxicity in mice
Antioxidant and hepatoprotective effects of Boswellia ovalifoliolata bark extracts
A high-yield and versatile method for the synthesis of carbon dots for bioimaging applications
Green preparation of carbon dots with mangosteen pulp for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions and cell imaging
Fluorescent carbon dots obtained from chitosan gel

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe3+ ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink
